---
title: "Homework4"
author: "Hemant"
date: "2024-11-27"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.



# Question 4


```{r}
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)

```

```{r}
# Define the folder path
folder_path <- "C:/Users/91734/Desktop/data_viz/Homework4/HW4_data"
```


```{r}
# Read the shapefile
tribal_regions <- st_read(paste0(folder_path, "/American_Indian_Reservations_in_Arizona.shp"))
```


```{r}
# View the structure and summary
print(tribal_regions)
summary(tribal_regions)

```


```{r}
# Question 2

#Arizona state of counties. Reproduce the figure below.

# Load required libraries
library(tigris)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
library(dplyr)

# Ensure shapefiles are downloaded only once
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# Download the shapefile for Arizona counties (2021)
arizona_counties <- counties(state = "AZ", year = 2021, progress_bar = FALSE)

# Prepare the county names and geometry for plotting
arizona_counties <- arizona_counties %>%
  mutate(NAME = gsub(" County", "", NAME))

# Convert shapefile to sf object for ggplot2 compatibility
arizona_counties_sf <- st_as_sf(arizona_counties)

# Extract centroid coordinates for labels
centroids <- arizona_counties_sf %>%
  st_centroid() %>%
  st_coordinates() %>%
  as.data.frame()

# Add coordinates back to the data
arizona_counties_sf <- arizona_counties_sf %>%
  mutate(longitude = centroids$X, latitude = centroids$Y)

# Plot the counties with labels
ggplot(data = arizona_counties_sf) +
  geom_sf(fill = "lightgrey", color = "black") +  # County boundaries
  geom_label(
    aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, label = NAME),
    size = 3,  # Adjust text size
    label.size = 0.2,  # Border thickness of the text box
    label.padding = unit(0.15, "lines"),  # Padding within text box
    label.r = unit(0.2, "lines")  # Rounded corners
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Counties in Arizona State",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude",
    caption = "Source: Shapefile obtained using {tigris} R package, v2.0.1"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),  # Center the title
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0)  # Left-align the caption
  )
```


```{r}
# Question 3

#Arizona state of population change. Reproduce the figure below.

# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(tigris)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(scales)

# Cache the shapefile to avoid re-downloading
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# Download the shapefile for Arizona counties
arizona_counties <- counties(state = "AZ", year = 2021, progress_bar = FALSE)

# Clean county names: remove " County" from the names
arizona_counties <- arizona_counties %>%
  mutate(NAME = gsub(" County", "", NAME))

# Define the file path to the Excel file
file_path <- "C:/Users/91734/Desktop/data_viz/Homework4/HW4_data/co-est2023-pop-04.xlsx"


# Read the Excel file, skipping the first 4 rows to get to the data
population_data_raw <- read_excel(
  path = file_path,
  sheet = 1,  # Adjust if your data is on a different sheet
  skip = 4,   # Skip metadata rows at the top
  col_names = FALSE # No headers in the data
)

# Inspect the data structure to identify columns
head(population_data_raw)

# Clean and select relevant columns
population_data <- population_data_raw %>%
  # Filter rows where the first column starts with a dot (indicating valid counties)
  filter(grepl("^\\.", `...1`)) %>%
  # Select columns: County names and Population Change (adjust columns as necessary)
  select(
    County = `...1`,               # County names
    total_pop_change = `...6`      # Population change column (adjust if needed)
  ) %>%
  # Remove leading dots and trailing text like " County, Arizona"
  mutate(
    County = gsub("^\\.| County, Arizona$", "", County)
  )

# Check the cleaned data
print(population_data)

# Merge shapefile with population data
arizona_counties_merged <- arizona_counties %>%
  left_join(population_data, by = c("NAME" = "County"))

# Identify counties that did not match (optional, for debugging)
unmatched <- arizona_counties_merged %>%
  filter(is.na(total_pop_change)) %>%
  pull(NAME)

if (length(unmatched) > 0) {
  warning("The following counties have missing data: ", paste(unmatched, collapse = ", "))
}

# Ensure the data is an sf object for plotting
arizona_counties_sf <- st_as_sf(arizona_counties_merged)

# Plot the map with ggplot2
ggplot(data = arizona_counties_sf) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = total_pop_change), color = "white", size = 0.3) +  # Add boundaries and fill
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = rev(brewer.pal(11, "RdBu")),   # Use the RdBu palette
    name = "Population Change",            # Legend title
    labels = comma,                        # Format numbers with commas
    na.value = "grey80"                    # Handle missing values with grey
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Resident Population Change for Counties in AZ",
    subtitle = "July 01, 2020 to July 01, 2023",
    caption = "Source: Shapefile obtained using {tigris} R package, v2.0.1\nPopulation change data from the US Census Bureau",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +          # Clean theme
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.position = "right"
  )



```


```{r}
population_preview <- read_excel("C:/Users/91734/Desktop/data_viz/Homework4/HW4_data/co-est2023-pop-04.xlsx")
head(population_preview)
```





# Question 4


```{r}
# Load necessary libraries
library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)

# Read the shapefiles
tribal_regions <- st_read("American_Indian_Reservations_in_Arizona.shp")

# Standardize CRS to ensure alignment
st_crs(tribal_regions) <- st_crs(arizona_counties)

# Create a new sf object for tribal region boundaries with thicker lines
tribal_boundaries <- st_union(tribal_regions) %>%
  st_cast("MULTILINESTRING")

```


```{r}
# # Filter for specific tribes (e.g., Hopi Tribe and Navajo Nation)
# filtered_tribes <- tribal_regions %>%
#   filter(grepl("Tohono O’odham Nation", tolower(NAME), ignore.case = TRUE))  # Using grepl for case-insensitive matching
# filtered_tribes$NAME

# Filter for specific tribes (e.g., Hopi Tribe and Navajo Nation)
filtered_tribes <- tribal_regions %>%
  filter(NAME %in% c("Hopi Tribe", 
                     "Navajo Nation", 
                     "White Mountain Apache Tribe",
                     "San Carlos Apache Tribe",
                     "Tohono O’odham Nation"))

# Filter the data for the specific tribes
hopi_tribe <- tribal_regions %>% filter(NAME == "Hopi Tribe")
navajo_nation <- tribal_regions %>% filter(NAME == "Navajo Nation")
white_mountain_apache <- tribal_regions %>% filter(NAME == "White Mountain Apache Tribe")
san_carlos_apache <- tribal_regions %>% filter(NAME == "San Carlos Apache Tribe")



ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = arizona_counties, color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  geom_sf(data = tribal_boundaries, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1) +
  # Add layer for Hopi Tribe with a distinct color (e.g., red)
  geom_sf(data = hopi_tribe, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # Add layer for Navajo Nation with a distinct color (e.g., blue)
  geom_sf(data = navajo_nation, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # Add layer for White Mountain Apache Tribe with a distinct color (e.g., green)
  geom_sf(data = white_mountain_apache, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # Add layer for San Carlos Apache Tribe with a distinct color (e.g., purple)
  geom_sf(data = san_carlos_apache, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +

  geom_label_repel(data = filtered_tribes,   # Add labels with arrows
                   aes(label = NAME, geometry = geometry),
                   stat = "sf_coordinates",  # Use centroids for label positioning
                   size = 3, fontface = "bold",
                   fill = "white", color = "black", # White label box, black text
                   label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"),  # Padding inside the label box
                   label.size = 0.3,                  # Border size of the label box
                   arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.02, "npc")),  # Add arrows
                   force = 10,                        # Adjust label repulsion force
                   max.overlaps = Inf                 # Allow max separation
  ) +
  # Or, if geom_text_repel doesn't work, try manual label placement with geom_text
  # geom_text(data = tribal_regions, aes(label = NAME, geometry = geometry),
  #           size = 3, nudge_x = 30000, nudge_y = 30000) +
  labs(
    title = "Indigenous Tribal Boundaries in AZ",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude",
    caption = "Source : Shapefile obtained using {tigris} R package, v2.0.1 \nIndigenous TribeShapefile obtained from AZGeo Data") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 10),  # Show axis text (adjust size)
        axis.ticks = element_line(),  # Show axis ticks
        plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 1)
        )
```


```{r}
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)

# Specify the path to the Excel file
file_path <- "co-est2023-pop-04.xlsx"

# Read the Excel file and skip the first two rows (header and description)
pop_data <- read_excel(file_path, skip = 4, n_max = 15)

# Change the column names
colnames(pop_data) <- c("Arizona County", "April 1st 2020", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023")

pop_data <- pop_data %>%
  mutate('Arizona County' = gsub(" County, Arizona", "", `Arizona County`)) # Remove "County" from population data 

# View the data to ensure it is read correctly
pop_data
```




```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

# Clean the county names in pop_data to match arizona_counties
pop_data$`Arizona County` <- str_remove(pop_data$`Arizona County`, "^\\.")  # Remove leading period
pop_data$`Arizona County` <- str_remove(pop_data$`Arizona County`, " County, Arizona$")  # Remove 'County, Arizona'

# Ensure consistent case formatting (both to title case)
pop_data$`Arizona County` <- str_to_title(pop_data$`Arizona County`)

# Perform the merge
merged_data <- left_join(arizona_counties, pop_data, by = c("NAME" = "Arizona County"))

# View the merged data
head(merged_data)
```

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

# Clean the county names in pop_data to match arizona_counties
pop_data$`Arizona County` <- str_remove(pop_data$`Arizona County`, "^\\.")  # Remove leading period
pop_data$`Arizona County` <- str_remove(pop_data$`Arizona County`, " County, Arizona$")  # Remove 'County, Arizona'

# Ensure consistent case formatting (both to title case)
pop_data$`Arizona County` <- str_to_title(pop_data$`Arizona County`)

# Perform the merge
merged_data <- left_join(arizona_counties, pop_data, by = c("NAME" = "Arizona County"))

# View the merged data
head(merged_data)
```
```{r}
# Calculate the total population change from 2021 to 2022
merged_data_diff <- merged_data %>%
  mutate(total_pop_change_21_22 = `2023` - `2020`)
```



```{r}
library(patchwork)

# Filter for specific tribes (e.g., Hopi Tribe and Navajo Nation)
filtered_az_counties <- arizona_counties %>%
  filter(NAME %in% c("Maricopa", "Pinal", "Pima"))


# Plot the map using a diverging RdBu color palette
base_map <- ggplot(data = merged_data_diff) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = total_pop_change_21_22), color = "white") +  # Color counties based on population change
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#08609e", mid = "white", high = "brown", 
                       midpoint = 70000, name = "Population Change") +  # Diverging color scale
  theme_minimal() +
  geom_label_repel(data = filtered_az_counties,   # Add labels with arrows
                   aes(label = NAME, geometry = geometry),
                   stat = "sf_coordinates",  # Use centroids for label positioning
                   size = 3, fontface = "bold",
                   fill = "white", color = "black", # White label box, black text
                   label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"),  # Padding inside the label box
                   label.size = 0.3,                  # Border size of the label box
                   arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.02, "npc")),  # Add arrows
                   force = 10,                        # Adjust label repulsion force
                   max.overlaps = Inf                 # Allow max separation
  ) +
  labs(title = "Resident Population Change for Counties in AZ",
       subtitle = "July 01, 2020 to July 01, 2023",
       x = "Longitude",
       y = "Latitude",
       caption = "Source: Shapefile obtained using {tigris} R package, v2.0.1 \npopulation change data from the US Census Bureau \nIndigenous Tribe Shapefile btained from AZGeo Data") +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 10),  # Show axis text (adjust size)
        axis.ticks = element_line(),  # Show axis ticks
        plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 1),
        legend.position.inside = c(0.25, 0.9),
       # Move legend to the top-left corner
        legend.direction = "horizontal",     # Set legend items horizontally
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA),  # Optional: background
        legend.title = element_text(size = 10),  # Style the legend title
        legend.text = element_text(size = 8),  # Style the legend text
        # legend.title.align = 0.5,  # Center the legend title
        legend.title.position = "top",
        legend.position = "top",
        legend.spacing.y = unit(0.3, "cm"),  # Increase space between the title and the legend keys
        legend.key.size = unit(0.7, "cm"),    # Control overall size of legend key
        legend.key.width = unit(0.95, "cm"),     # Increase the width of the legend key
        legend.key.height = unit(0.4, "cm")   # Decrease the height of the legend key
        ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-122, -109), ylim = c(29, 37))  # Adjust x-axis and y-axis range


```



```{r}
# Filter for specific tribes (e.g., Hopi Tribe and Navajo Nation)
filtered_tribes_sub <- tribal_regions %>%
  filter(NAME %in% c("White Mountain Apache Tribe",
                     "San Carlos Apache Tribe",
                     "Tohono O’odham Nation"))

# Plot the map using a diverging RdBu color palette
inset_map <- ggplot(data = merged_data_diff) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = total_pop_change_21_22), color = "white") +  # Color counties based on population change
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#08609e", mid = "white", high = "brown", guide = "none",
                       midpoint = 70000, name = "Population Change") +  # Diverging color scale
  geom_sf(data = tribal_boundaries, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1) +
  # Add layer for Hopi Tribe with a distinct color (e.g., red)
  geom_sf(data = hopi_tribe, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # Add layer for Navajo Nation with a distinct color (e.g., blue)
  geom_sf(data = navajo_nation, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # Add layer for White Mountain Apache Tribe with a distinct color (e.g., green)
  geom_sf(data = white_mountain_apache, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # Add layer for San Carlos Apache Tribe with a distinct color (e.g., purple)
  geom_sf(data = san_carlos_apache, fill = "gray", color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.3) +
  
  # labs(title = "Resident Population Change for Counties in AZ",
  #      subtitle = "July 01, 2020 to July 01, 2023",
  #      x = "Longitude",
  #      y = "Latitude",
  #      caption = "Source: Shapefile obtained using {tigris} R package, v2.0.1 \npopulation change data from the US Census Bureau") +
  # theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 10),  # Show axis text (adjust size)
  #       axis.ticks = element_line(),  # Show axis ticks
  #       plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
  #       plot.caption = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0.5)
  #       ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-113, -110.5), ylim = c(31.5, 34)) + # Adjust coordinates as needed
  # theme(plot.margin = unit(c(1, 3, 0.5, 0.5), "cm")) + # Add space around the plot for the background
  geom_label_repel(data = filtered_tribes_sub,   # Add labels with arrows
                   aes(label = NAME, geometry = geometry),
                   stat = "sf_coordinates",  # Use centroids for label positioning
                   size = 1.8, fontface = "bold",
                   fill = "white", color = "black", # White label box, black text
                   label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"),  # Padding inside the label box
                   label.size = 0.3,                  # Border size of the label box
                   arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.02, "npc")),  # Add arrows
                   force = 10,                        # Adjust label repulsion force
                   max.overlaps = Inf                 # Allow max separation
  )+
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),  # Remove axis titles
    axis.text = element_blank(),  # Remove axis text
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),  # Remove axis ticks
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA),  # Set background color for the entire plot
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA)  # Set background color for the panel (map area)
  )
  

```



```{r}
library(ggplot2)

# Assuming you have the base_map and inset_map already defined.

# Create the base_map with additional lines
base_map_with_lines <- base_map +
  # Example dotted line from a point in base map to inset (adjust coordinates as needed)
  geom_segment(aes(x = -113, y = 32, xend = -118, yend = 32), linetype = "dotted", size = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = -113, y = 32, xend = -118, yend = 30), linetype = "dotted", size = 0.7, color = "black") +
  theme(plot.margin = unit(c(0, 0, 0, 0), "cm"))  # Remove margins

compressed_inset <- inset_map +
  theme(plot.margin = unit(c(0.3,0.3,0.1,0.1), "cm"))  # Remove margins

# Combine with scaled inset
combined_plot <- base_map_with_lines +
  inset_element(
    compressed_inset,
    left = -0.2,
    bottom = 0,
    right = .6,
    top = .5
  )

# Display the combined plot with dotted lines
combined_plot
```
